findings: If the ovaHRDscar score was 0, the result was considered unreliable and was excluded from further analyses. HGSC is characterized by a significant amount of genomic instability, and if indeed the evaluated sum of LOH, LSTs and nTAIs is 0, this is suspicious of a false negative result.

In addition to the previously described prospective fHR samples, we identified 27 retrospective patients treated with PARPi in the 2nd line and subjected them to fHR testing from primary tumor samples, with the aim of evaluating how fHR predicts PARPi responses in the 2nd line.
risks_or_open_items: Surgical samples from 50 patients were also processed for WES and RNA-seq. Additionally, samples were processed into formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue blocks, which were used for fHRD testing in the 2nd line PARPi treated patient cohort.

document_index.title: FF DNA extraction Maxwell ElinaNiemelä
document_index.excerpt: DNA from fresh-frozen tissue samples was extracted using Maxwell CSC Genomic DNA automated bead-based extraction kit. Briefly, up to 50 mg of tissue was shredded using a scalpel, transferred to a clean Eppendorf tube, and lysed according to the manufacturer’s instructions for 2 hours on a thermal mixer set to 56°C and 1400 rpm. Automated extraction was performed according to instructions and DNA was eluted to 100 µl low-EDTA TE. DNA quality was monitored using spectrophotometric and fluorometric assays.

document_index.title: Functional HR testing
document_index.excerpt: Three serial FFPE sections of each tumor specimen were deparaffinized in xylene and rehydrated in decreasing concentrations of ethanol. Sections were boiled in 10 mM citrate buffer, pH 6, for antigen retrieval, and immunostained according to standard procedures with primary antibodies against cytokeratin (panCK, C2931, Sigma Aldrich), γH2Ax (CST9718S, Cell Signaling Technology®), geminin (ab104306, Abcam), and RAD51 (ab133534, Abcam) in three combinations: panCK & γH2Ax, geminin & γH2Ax, and geminin & RAD51. Sections were then incubated with fluorescently labeled secondary antibodies (donkey anti-mouse IgG-Alexa-Fluor 488TM, A-21202, InvitrogenTM; and donkey anti-rabbit IgG-Alexa-Fluor 647TM, A-31573, InvitrogenTM) and nuclei were counterstained with Hoechst 33342 (InvitrogenTM). Analysis of PARPi samples in Tartu - May 2025
A batch of 94 samples were sent to Tartu for SNP-array analysis using the Illumina GSA v3. The results from the genotyping unit in Tartu were deposited in a server by them. The access to the server was shared in an email to Matilda. The Tartu’s genomic unit included a new cluster file and a new manifest file, because this SNP array version had a bit less SNPs than the ones used before in FIMM-Helsinki.
The SNP-array data and cluster and manifest files were downloaded and organized in folders by Fernando. The data was analysed using the same GenomeStudio protocol, with the new cluster and manifest files proportioned by Tartu. The new pipeline for SNP-array analysis was then executed (https://github.com/farkkilab/SNParray_ovaHRDscar_QC).

Real-world data from the Oncosys-OVA clinical trial (NCT06117384) - experiences on genomic and functional HRD testing and molecular determinants of progression during PARP inhibitor treatment 
Anni Härkönen1, Matilda Salko1, Fernando Perez1, Sanna Pikkusaari1, Anni Virtanen1,3, Heini  
Lassus1,2, Liisa Kauppi1, Ulla-Maija Haltia1,2, Anniina Färkkilä1,2,4 
Research Program in Systems Oncology, University of Helsinki, Faculty of Medicine, Finland 
Department of Oncology and Obstetrics and Gynecology, Helsinki University Hospital, Helsinki,  
Finland 
Department of Pathology, University of Helsinki and HUS Diagnostic Center,  
Helsinki University Hospital, Finland 
Institute for Molecular Medicine Finland, Helsinki Institute of Life Sciences, University of Helsinki, Finland 
 
Background 
Identifying high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSC) patients who derive the greatest benefit from poly (ADP-ribose) polymerase inhibitor (PARPi) therapy is crucial. This study aims to establish optimal homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) testing strategies and to characterise the molecular features underlying disease progression in HRD patients receiving first-line PARPi maintenance treatment. 
Methods  
We analysed a cohort of 323 HGSC patients who underwent various HRD testing modalities at different treatment stages, with clinical outcomes monitored through a comprehensive database. Genomic HRD status was assessed in 251 patients using the ovaHRDscar algorithm with a tumour purity threshold of ≥30%. Two PARPi-treated sub-cohorts were also examined.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: Manuscript/Abstracts for conferences/ESGO 2026 abstract AH.docx::chunk_0001
vector_chunks.source_file: Manuscript/Abstracts for conferences/ESGO 2026 abstract AH.docx
vector_chunks.text:
atment stages, with clinical outcomes monitored through a comprehensive database. Genomic HRD status was assessed in 251 patients using the ovaHRDscar algorithm with a tumour purity threshold of ≥30%. Two PARPi-treated sub-cohorts were also examined. The first comprised 28 second-line PARPi-treated patients, evaluated using RAD51-based functional HRD (fHRD) testing. The second included 44 patients treated with first-line PARPi who underwent whole-exome sequencing (WES) and RNA sequencing (RNA-seq) for mutational and transcriptomic profiling. 
Results 
Real-world data from 154 evaluable patients demonstrated significantly longer first-line progression-free survival (PFS) among HRD patients. Genomic HRD testing of neoadjuvant chemotherapy (NACT)-treated samples failed in 36% of cases due to low tumour purity (<30%), with failure rates increasing from 28% in patients who received three NACT cycles to 38% in those who received four. Analysis of 33 pre-NACT biopsy samples confirmed that core-needle biopsies are sufficient and safe for HRD testing. Moreover, fHRD testing was feasible for small biopsy samples and those with low tumour purity. Importantly, fHRD was associated with prolonged PFS in patients treated with second-line PARPi maintenance therapy. Ongoing RNA-seq and WES analyses for 44 HRD patients will reveal molecular underpinnings of progression during first-line PARPi treatment. 
Our study provides new insights into HRD testing strategies and establishes molecular determinants of response and resistance to PARPi in real-world clinical samples.

Improved clinical HRD testing practices in ovarian cancer based on real-world data from the Oncosys-OVA clinical trial (NCT06117384)
Matilda Salko1, Anni Härkönen1, Fernando Perez1, Sanna Pikkusaari1, Anni Virtanen1,3, Heini Lassus1,2, Liisa Kauppi1, Ulla-Maija Haltia1,2, Anniina Färkkilä1,3,4
Research Program in Systems Oncology, University of Helsinki, Faculty of Medicine, Finland
Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, Helsinki University Hospital, Helsinki, Finland
Department of Pathology, University of Helsinki and HUS Diagnostic Center, Helsinki University Hospital, Finland
Institute for Molecular Medicine Finland, Helsinki Institute of Life Sciences, University of Helsinki, Finland
Recent advances in high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSC) treatment have been driven by the availability of clinical testing for homologous recombination deficiency (HRD), determining patients who most benefit from maintenance therapy with poly (ADP-ribose) polymerase inhibitors (PARPi). However, questions regarding the optimal approach to HRD testing in clinical practice remain, including the selection of optimal material and timing for tumor tissue. We have previously observed that HRD testing from interval debulking surgery samples after neo-adjuvant chemotherapy is frequently unsuccesful, usually due to insufficient tumor purity (<30%). These patients, however, typically show an excellent response to DNA-damaging chemotherapy, suggesting HRD. 
We assembled a cohort of 400 HGSC patients subjected to different HRD testing modalities at different time points and monitored for clinical outcomes. Clinical HRD testing in Finland currently utilizes fresh-frozen tumor material. We analyzed 92 FFPE samples to optimize FFPE-sample based ovaHRDscar testing.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: Manuscript/Abstracts for conferences/ESMO abstract MS 2025-05-13.docx::chunk_0001
vector_chunks.source_file: Manuscript/Abstracts for conferences/ESMO abstract MS 2025-05-13.docx
vector_chunks.text:
fferent HRD testing modalities at different time points and monitored for clinical outcomes. Clinical HRD testing in Finland currently utilizes fresh-frozen tumor material. We analyzed 92 FFPE samples to optimize FFPE-sample based ovaHRDscar testing. Importantly, we also analyzed 94 tumor samples from patients who were treated with PARPi based on a platinum sensitive relapse or BRCA mutation before the implementation of HRD testing.
From 14 paired biopsy and surgical samples, we demonstrate that HRD testing from a small, minimally invasive pre-chemotherapy biopsy is both safe and effective, with a 67% success rate. Additionally, we complement the genomic HRD results with a RAD51-based functional HRD (fHRD) assay and outline when fHRD is preferred over genomic testing. Our retrospective sub-cohort of PARPi treated patients will provide insights into long-term PARPi responses after recurrence and potentially identify other factors influencing PARPi response beyond HRD. In addition to these novel insights, we also present full follow-up data from 201 patients subjected to HRD testing from surgical samples upon diagnosis.
In conclusion, our study offers new insights into optimal HRD testing strategies. Additionally, we report how HRD testing predicts real-world PARPi responses, and whether there are other factors affecting these responses beyond HRD.

Improved clinical HRD testing practices in ovarian cancer based on real-world data from the Oncosys-OVA clinical trial (NCT06117384)
Matilda Salko1, Anni Härkönen1, Fernando Perez1, Sanna Pikkusaari1, Elina NiemeläX, Anni Virtanen1,3, Heini Lassus1,2, Liisa Kauppi1, Ulla-Maija Haltia1,2, Anniina Färkkilä1,3,4
To be added: Sara Palomino-Echeverria, Andreas Hainari, iCAN
Research Program in Systems Oncology, University of Helsinki, Faculty of Medicine, Finland
Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, Helsinki University Hospital, Helsinki, Finland
Department of Pathology, University of Helsinki and HUS Diagnostic Center, Helsinki University Hospital, Finland
Institute for Molecular Medicine Finland, Helsinki Institute of Life Sciences, University of Helsinki, Finland 
Introduction
Poly (ADP-ribose) polymerase inhibitors (PARPi) have revolutionized the treatment of high-grade serous carcinomas (HGSC), of which 50% harbor homologous recombination deficiency (HRD), making them susceptible to PARPi treatment [ref Konstantinopoulos, ref TCGA]. The availability of PARPi as first-line maintenance treatment makes the early and accurrate determination of patients’ HRD status a clinically important feat [ref Ray-Coquard, ref González-Martín; PAOLA-1/PRIMA]. Both commercially available tests (e.g. Myriad MyChoice CDx) and several open-source laboratory-developed tests (LDTs) have become available to meet this need [ref Quesada et al]. 
In Finland, clinical HRD testing has been standard practice for all patients with newly diagnosed HGSC or high-grade endometrioid ovarian cancer since 2022. The test is conducted from fresh-frozen tissue samples collected in either primary debulking surgery (PDS), or interval debulking surgery (IDS) after neoadjuvant chemotherapy (NACT).

vector_chunks.source_file: Manuscript/Improved clinical HRD testing practices in ovarian cancer manuscript.docx
vector_chunks.text:
iagnosed HGSC or high-grade endometrioid ovarian cancer since 2022. The test is conducted from fresh-frozen tissue samples collected in either primary debulking surgery (PDS), or interval debulking surgery (IDS) after neoadjuvant chemotherapy (NACT). The results are obtained by using an ovarian cancer optimized algorithm for HRD detection, ovaHRDscar, which evaluates the genomic instability of the tumor by assessing loss of heterozygosity (LOH), large-scale state transitions (LSTs) and telomeric allelic imbalances (TAIs) [ref Perez-Villatoro]. 
The ESMO-ESGO-ESP consensus recommends that HRD testing should be performed from either a surgical sample or image-guided biopsy of treatment-naive tumor [ref Ledermann, ESMO-ESGO-ESP consensus]. However, until now there have been no publications on the effect NACT treatment has on HRD testing and whether IDS samples can be used for HRD testing, or if indeed biopsy samples are feasible and reliable for this purpose. Besides genomic HRD tests, RAD51-staining based assays to evaluate the functional HR status have emerged, and especially show potential for determining the HR status of tumor samples with low tumor purity [ref Pikkusaari, ref Quesada]. However, no current recommendations for using functional testing in the clinical setting exist. 
Although PARPi treatment has significantly improved the treatment of HGSC patients with HRD tumors, it is no silver bullet. A large subset of patients with confirmed HRD tumors still experience cancer progression during PARPi maintenance treatment.

Although PARPi treatment has significantly improved the treatment of HGSC patients with HRD tumors, it is no silver bullet. A large subset of patients with confirmed HRD tumors still experience cancer progression during PARPi maintenance treatment. It is still unclear why this happens, and we set out to elucidate this phenomenon with the help of whole-exome sequencing (WES) and RNA sequencing (RNA-seq) from first-line PARPi treated patients, with the aim of uncovering molecular features underlying disease progression. 
Materials and methods
Patients
Our cohort consisted of XXX ovarian cancer patients enrolled in the observational, prospective clinical trial Oncosys-OVA (NCT06117384) at the Helsinki University Hospital (HUS) Women’s Clinic between 2019-2024. The study was performed in accordance with the ethical standards from the 1975 Declaration of Helsinki. A written informed consent was obtained from all study participants and the use of samples and clinical data was approved by the local Ethics Committee (HUS334/2021). 
The full cohort composition is illustrated in Figure 1A. Patients with either a high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) or endometrioid ovarian cancer G3 histology were included in the study. Patients were clinically tested for HRD with the ovaHRDscar algorithm from fresh-frozen tumor tissue samples collected in PDS or IDS surgery, and 34 of the NACT+IDS treated patients were also HRD tested from pre-treatment tissue biopsies. 
 There were 27 patients in the cohort who received PARPi in the 2nd treatment line before the implementation of clinical HRD testing.

vector_chunks.source_file: Manuscript/Improved clinical HRD testing practices in ovarian cancer manuscript.docx
vector_chunks.text:
DS or IDS surgery, and 34 of the NACT+IDS treated patients were also HRD tested from pre-treatment tissue biopsies. 
 There were 27 patients in the cohort who received PARPi in the 2nd treatment line before the implementation of clinical HRD testing. These patients were diagnosed between 20XX-20YY, when the eligibility for PARPi treatment was based on either a somatic or genomic BRCA mutation, or a platinum sensitive (platinum-free interval [PFI] > 6 months) disease relapse. These patients were subjected to functional HRD testing from tissue FFPE blocks to determine (a) the feasibility of fHRD testing in the clinical setting and (b) real-world responses to PARPi in the 2nd line. Due to the unavailability of fresh-frozen tissue samples from these patients, they were not HRD tested with the ovaHRDscar algorithm. 
 Additionally, we identified 50 patients who had received PARPi as first line maintenance treatment, of which n=XX had completed the full two years of maintenance treatment, and n=YY had progressed during PARPi treatment. These patients were then subjected to WES and RNAseq for mutational and transcriptomic profiling, with the aim of characterising the molecular features underlying disease progression in HRD patients receiving first-line PARPi maintenance treatment. 
Surgical samples
Fresh-frozen tissue samples were collected during PDS or IDS surgery for research, diagnostic and genomic analysis purposes. Samples collected for clinical purposes were processed for ovaHRDscar testing at the Helsinki University Hospital central laboratory HUSLAB. DNA from fresh-frozen tissue samples was extracted using Maxwell CSC Genomic DNA automated bead-based extraction kit.

vector_chunks.text:
collected for clinical purposes were processed for ovaHRDscar testing at the Helsinki University Hospital central laboratory HUSLAB. DNA from fresh-frozen tissue samples was extracted using Maxwell CSC Genomic DNA automated bead-based extraction kit. Briefly, up to 50 mg of tissue was shredded using a scalpel, transferred to a clean Eppendorf tube, and lysed according to the manufacturer’s instructions for 2 hours on a thermal mixer set to 56°C and 1400 rpm. Automated extraction was performed according to instructions and DNA was eluted to 100 µl low-EDTA TE. DNA quality was monitored using spectrophotometric and fluorometric assays. Samples collected for research purposes were processed in our research laboratory for genomic HRD testing with ovaHRDscar according to the same protocol used by HUSLAB. 
Although fresh-frozen samples are used for the clinical HRD testing purposes in Finland, Korsholm et al. have also demonstrated the feasibility of using ovaHRDscar in evaluating the HRD status from FFPE samples using the OncoScan CNV assay [ref Korsholm]. We show in Supplementary Figure X that quality controlled FFPE samples analyzed with Illumina SNParray also have a good concordance with paired results from the commercial Myriad MyChoice CDx assay. Thus, if no fresh-frozen sample result was available, we have also used results from quality controlled FFPE samples (n=XX) in our analyses. 
Surgical samples from 50 patients were also processed for WES and RNA-seq. Additionally, samples were processed into formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue blocks, which were used for fHRD testing in the 2nd line PARPi treated patient cohort.

Pre-NACT HRD testing was performed on 34 core needle biopsy samples (Table X).

paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue blocks, which were used for fHRD testing in the 2nd line PARPi treated patient cohort. 

Ultrasound-guided pre-chemotherapy biopsies Anni H
Pre-NACT HRD testing was performed on 34 core needle biopsy samples (Table X). The aim was to obtain pre-treatment biopsies from all patients for whom NACT+IDS was planned between October 2022 and December 2024. If the biopsy yielded a HRD positive result, it was accepted as such, but if the result was HRD negative, a corresponding sample was collected from IDS. However, sample acquisition from surgery was not possible for all patients, mainly due to excellent NACT responses. The pre-NACT biopsies were collected either with US-guided (91%, n=31) or CT-guided (3%, n=1) biopsy, or in the case of 2 patients (6%) in a diagnostic laparoscopy. The biopsies were obtained by an experienced radiologist, and the laparoscopies were performed by an experienced surgeon specialised in gynecologic oncology. 
17.6% (n=6) of the patients had received one cycle of NACT before the biopsy sample was taken. The time between the first NACT cycle and the biopsy procedure varied from 1 day to 8 days. From 82.4% (n=28) of the patients, the sample was collected before the first NACT cycle. 
Sample tumor purity analysis
The required sample tumor purity for obtaining a reliable HRD test result is > 30% [ref Romey]. The tumor purities of the surgical samples in this study were assessed from the same tissue block from which the HRD test was conducted by an experienced pathologist. However, regarding the thick needle biopsies the tumor purity could not routinely be assessed from the same core that was used for HRD testing.

e assessed from the same tissue block from which the HRD test was conducted by an experienced pathologist. However, regarding the thick needle biopsies the tumor purity could not routinely be assessed from the same core that was used for HRD testing. Because the tumor cell content from an adjacent core is not representative of the tested samples’ tumor content, we refrained from using tumor purity as a quality metric for biopsy samples. 
Genomic HRD testing with ovaHRDscar Anni H
Genomic HRD testing using ovaHRDscar was performed on a DNA isolated from the collected tissue samples and profiled using Illumina global screening array. First, B allele frequency (BAF), log R ratio (LRR) and call rates were calculated for each sample from the SNP array using Illumina-GenomeStudio version 2.0. Quality control was assessed for each array using call rates and the median of the absolute values of all pairwise differences (MAPD) of LRRs. ASCAT estimates tumor purity and ploidy for each sample to identify cases with abnormal tumor cell content or genome in the data. By integrating this information with the BAF and LRR signals, ASCAT detects samples with unreliable or low-quality data, which are subsequently excluded from further analysis. Samples with Call rate values below 0.90 and MAPD greater than 0.30 [ref, Affymetrix] were considered low quality and excluded from further analysis. Allele-spesific copy number profiles were inferred using ASCAT software version 3.2.0. HRDscars were then calculated for each sample using the ovaHRDscar algorithm.
 If the ovaHRDscar score was 0, the result was considered unreliable and was excluded from further analyses.

 If the ovaHRDscar score was 0, the result was considered unreliable and was excluded from further analyses. HGSC is characterized by a significant amount of genomic instability, and if indeed the evaluated sum of LOH, LSTs and nTAIs is 0, this is suspicious of a false negative result. 
Functional HRD testing Sanna P
Three serial FFPE sections of each tumor specimen were deparaffinized in xylene and rehydrated in decreasing concentrations of ethanol. Sections were boiled in 10 mM citrate buffer, pH 6, for antigen retrieval, and immunostained according to standard procedures with primary antibodies against cytokeratin (panCK, C2931, Sigma Aldrich), γH2Ax (CST9718S, Cell Signaling Technology®), geminin (ab104306, Abcam), and RAD51 (ab133534, Abcam) in three combinations: panCK & γH2Ax, geminin & γH2Ax, and geminin & RAD51. Sections were then incubated with fluorescently labeled secondary antibodies (donkey anti-mouse IgG-Alexa-Fluor 488TM, A-21202, InvitrogenTM; and donkey anti-rabbit IgG-Alexa-Fluor 647TM, A-31573, InvitrogenTM) and nuclei were counterstained with Hoechst 33342 (InvitrogenTM). Slides were digitized using Pannoramic 250 FLASH III slide scanner (3DHISTECH) at 20x magnification. 
Functional HR (fHR) scoring was performed as described previously by Pikkusaari et al. [ref Pikkusaari]. Briefly, regions of interest (ROIs) from each of the three staining combinations were analyzed in FIJI/ImageJ using custom macros. ROIs with high tumor cell content, marked by panCK, and with visible DNA damage, marked by γH2Ax, were chosen for analysis based on the panCK-γH2Ax–stained serial section.

three staining combinations were analyzed in FIJI/ImageJ using custom macros. ROIs with high tumor cell content, marked by panCK, and with visible DNA damage, marked by γH2Ax, were chosen for analysis based on the panCK-γH2Ax–stained serial section. From the geminin-γH2Ax–stained section, the percentage of S-G2 phase nuclei (geminin positive) with DNA damage (γH2Ax positive) was quantified. The fHR score was obtained from geminin-RAD51–stained sections as the percentage of S-G2 phase nuclei undergoing HR-mediated repair, i.e., RAD51-positive nuclei, out of all S-G2 phase nuclei. As per previously established cut-off values, chemo-naïve samples with fHR score of <10 were classified as functionally HR-deficient, whereas samples with fHR score of >10 were classified as functionally HR-proficient. For chemo-treated samples, a cut-off of <30 was used for HR deficiency.

Statistics
Progression-free survival was defined as the time between cancer diagnosis and the first incidence of disease progression or death from any cause. Progression was evaluated from a computed tomography (CT) scan according to the RECIST 1.1 criteria for solid tumors, or clinically e.g. as the appearance of malignant pleural fluid. For patients treated with PARPi in the second treatment line after the first disease progression, the duration of PARPi treatment, determined as the time between the PARPi treatment initiation and the next subsequent disease progression, was used to evaluate the responsiveness to PARPi instead of PFS.

Results
36% of IDS samples have insufficient tumor purity for HRD testing 
First, we set out to analyze the differences between samples collected from PDS and IDS, and the possible effects of surgery type on the success of genomic HRD testing. In samples collected from PDS (n=184) the tumor purity was sufficient for HRD testing (> 30%) in 96.7% (n=178) of the samples (Figure 1B). In samples collected from IDS after NACT exposure (n=92), the tumor purity was > 30% in only 64.1% (n=59) of the samples, resulting in 35.9% (n=33) of the samples having insufficient tumor purity for genomic HRD testing (Figure 1C). Obtaining an adequate sample with sufficient tumor purity from IDS was further affected by the number of NACT cycles the patient received before surgery. In patients who received 4 (n=70) or 5 (n=4) cycles of NACT, the tumor purity in the IDS surgery samples further diminished, with only 62.2% (n=46) having a tumor purity of >30%. In patients treated with 3 (n=17) or 2 (n=1) cycles of NACT, the IDS sample tumor purity was improved compared to the NACT+IDS subgroup as a whole, with 72.2% (n=13) having a tumor purity of > 30% (Figure 1D-E). 
Pre-NACT biopsies are feasible and reliable for genomic HRD testing 
Due to the frequency of low tumor purity samples from IDS surgery that were unfit for HRD testing, we set out to assess the feasibility of using pre-treatment biopsies for HRD testing in this patient group. 34 biopsies were collected, of which 32 were image-guided and 2 laparoscopic (Table X).

ples from IDS surgery that were unfit for HRD testing, we set out to assess the feasibility of using pre-treatment biopsies for HRD testing in this patient group. 34 biopsies were collected, of which 32 were image-guided and 2 laparoscopic (Table X). 85% (n=29) of all biopsies yielded an ovaHRDscar score > 0, which was considered a successful result. If the biopsy was unsuccessful or the test result HRD negative, the aim was to collect a corresponding sample from IDS surgery for a repeated HRD test from the same patient. 15 sample pairs with both a pre-NACT biopsy and corresponding IDS surgery sample were collected (Table X). Out of these sample pairs, 87% (n=13) of the biopsy samples yielded a successful HRD test result, compared to only 73% (n=11) of the corresponding IDS surgery samples (Figure 1E). 
In addition to the paired samples, 19 biopsy-only samples were collected. Out of these, 44% (n=8) were HRD positive and 56% (n=10) HRD negative. For the HRD positive samples, the tissue sample extracted in surgery was sent to the pathology unit for assessment of tumor purity. Only 50% (n=4) of the corresponding IDS surgery samples would have had a sufficient tumor purity (>30%) for HRD testing from the surgical sample. Regarding the 10 biopsy-only but HRD negative samples, a surgical sample was not collected in 60% (n=6) of the cases, since there was no vital tumor left in IDS. For all these six patients, chemotherapy response score (CRS) was 3, as assessed by an experienced pathologist from the surgical samples. To obtain a HRD result, a functional HRD test was performed for three of these patients. For the rest of the patients, Table X depicts why further HRD testing was not performed.

Functional HRD testing can be utilized on real-world samples where genomic testing has been unsuccessful
As described earlier, in situations where tumor samples had insufficient purity for genomic HRD testing, a fHR test was performed. We collected 13 such samples, and followed for clinical treatment responses. 61.5% (n=8) of the samples were functionally HRD (fHRD) and 38.5% (n=5) functionally HRP (fHRP). fHRD patients were eligible for PARPi treatment in the first line. ll patients had FIGO stage III-VI disease.92.3% (n=12) of the patients while 7.7% (n=1) had HGSC combined with high grade adenocarcinoma. 69.2% (n=9) patients received NACT followed by IDS, 7.7% (n=1) of the patients underwent PDS and 23.1% (n=3) received chemotherapy alone. . 
PFS was evaluated as an exploratory manner excluding those who were not treated with debulking surgery and platinum-based chemotherapy. Two fHRD patients did not receive PARPi, and when evaluating PFS for PARPi-treated fHRD patients and fHRP patients, the median PFS were 16.0 and 15.4 months, respectively. The median OS of the PARPi-treated fHRD patients 16.9 months, compared with 15.4 months in fHRP patients suggest modest survival benefit in fHRD patients. However, it should be noted that the sample amount is very low.

Additionally, among the patients treated with chemotherapy (n=3), genomic testing was not performed as one had no FF sample available, and two yielded insufficient tumor purities in the biopsy samples. In conclusion, functional HRD testing was feasible and offered an important completion to genomic testing, especially for patients whose genomic testing from biopsy samples resulted unsuccessful and they either did not proceed to surgery or they responded well to platinum based chemotherapy, resulting in no available tissue sample from the surgery. 
Functional HRD testing identifies a trend for prolonged response to PARPi maintenance in the 2nd treatment line
In addition to the previously described prospective fHR samples, we identified 27 retrospective patients treated with PARPi in the 2nd line and subjected them to fHR testing from primary tumor samples, with the aim of evaluating how fHR predicts PARPi responses in the 2nd line. 
In this subset of patients, the median age at the time of diagnosis was 65 years (range 50-85), and all patients were of HGSC histology. 96.3% (n=26) presented with advanced (FIGO stage III-IV) disease, and 3.7% with FIGO stage IIA disease. Functional HRD testing was performed from FFPE samples collected from the patients at the time of the initial diagnosis in either PDS (85.2%, n=23), IDS (11.1%, n=3), or diagnostic laparoscopy (3.7%, n=1). Out of the 28 samples tested for functional HRD, XX% (n=YY) were fHRD, and XX% (n=YY) were fHRP. 
Real-world data shows prolonged PFS in HRD+ patients receiving PARPi treatment 
We analyzed the real-world outcomes of clinically HRD tested patients receiving first-line PARPi maintenance between September 2022 and December 2024.

Real-world data shows prolonged PFS in HRD+ patients receiving PARPi treatment 
We analyzed the real-world outcomes of clinically HRD tested patients receiving first-line PARPi maintenance between September 2022 and December 2024. The cohort consisted of XXX patients, and the cohort characteristics are described in Table X. 60% (n=93) of the patients were treated with upfront PDS followed by adjuvant chemotherapy, and 40% (n=61) with NACT followed by IDS and adjuvant chemotherapy. 
The patients underwent genomic BRCA and HRD testing from fresh-frozen tissue samples collected either from PDS or IDS surgery. Eligibility for first-line maintenance treatment with PARPi was based off a positive HRD test result, or a detected somatic or germline BRCA mutation. 82% (n=126) of the patients were BRCA wild-type, 7.8% (n=12) had a somatic BRCA mutation, and 9.7% (n=15) had a germline BRCA mutation. One patient had a germline BRCA mutation of unknown clinical significance. 43% (n=66) of all patients were HRD positive and 53% (n=81) HRD negative. 4.5% (n=7) of the HRD result was not available. Out of these 7, one patient did not have a HRD test done, one had discrepant results from two HRD tests (one HRD and the other HRP), 2 had insufficient tumor purity, and in 3 cases, the test could not be completed due to a technical issue [ASCAT fail?]. In cases (n=XX) where the HRD test result was negative but the patient had a detected BRCA mutation, the case was resolved in favor of the BRCA mutation and the patient was eligible for PARPi. 
PARPi maintenance was administered either as single treatment or, if applicable, in combination with 20 cycles of maintenance bevacizumab treatment. 41% (n=63) received PARPi treatment.

WES and RNA-seq reveals molecular features underlying disease progression in first-line PARPi treated patients with HRD 
To elucidate why some HRD patients, even including patients with BRCA mutations, experience disease progression during PARPi treatment, we analyzed XX patients treated with PARPi in the 1st treatment line. All patients were of HGSC histology and presented with advanced (FIGO stage III-IV) disease. XX% (n=YY) harbored a somatic BRCA1/2 mutation, and XX% (n=YY) were carriers of a germline BRCA1/2 mutation.

vector_chunks.text: DNA from fresh-frozen tissue samples was extracted using Maxwell CSC Genomic DNA automated bead-based extraction kit. Briefly, up to 50 mg of tissue was shredded using a scalpel, transferred to a clean Eppendorf tube, and lysed according to the manufacturer’s instructions for 2 hours on a thermal mixer set to 56°C and 1400 rpm. Automated extraction was performed according to instructions and DNA was eluted to 100 µl low-EDTA TE. DNA quality was monitored using spectrophotometric and fluorometric assays.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: Manuscript/Manuscript sections from co-authors/Functional HR testing_2025-10-23_SP.docx::chunk_0000

vector_chunks.text:
Functional HR testing
Three serial FFPE sections of each tumor specimen were deparaffinized in xylene and rehydrated in decreasing concentrations of ethanol. Sections were boiled in 10 mM citrate buffer, pH 6, for antigen retrieval, and immunostained according to standard procedures with primary antibodies against cytokeratin (panCK, C2931, Sigma Aldrich), γH2Ax (CST9718S, Cell Signaling Technology®), geminin (ab104306, Abcam), and RAD51 (ab133534, Abcam) in three combinations: panCK & γH2Ax, geminin & γH2Ax, and geminin & RAD51. Sections were then incubated with fluorescently labeled secondary antibodies (donkey anti-mouse IgG-Alexa-Fluor 488TM, A-21202, InvitrogenTM; and donkey anti-rabbit IgG-Alexa-Fluor 647TM, A-31573, InvitrogenTM) and nuclei were counterstained with Hoechst 33342 (InvitrogenTM). Slides were digitized using Pannoramic 250 FLASH III slide scanner (3DHISTECH) at 20x magnification. 
Functional HR (fHR) scoring was performed as described previously (Pikkusaari et al. 2023). Briefly, regions of interest (ROIs) from each of the three staining combinations were analyzed in FIJI/ImageJ using custom macros. ROIs with high tumor cell content, marked by panCK, and with visible DNA damage, marked by γH2Ax, were chosen for analysis based on the panCK-γH2Ax–stained serial section. From the geminin-γH2Ax–stained section, the percentage of S-G2 phase nuclei (geminin positive) with DNA damage (γH2Ax positive) was quantified. The fHR score was obtained from geminin-RAD51–stained sections as the percentage of S-G2 phase nuclei undergoing HR-mediated repair, i.e., RAD51-positive nuclei, out of all S-G2 phase nuclei.

positive) with DNA damage (γH2Ax positive) was quantified. The fHR score was obtained from geminin-RAD51–stained sections as the percentage of S-G2 phase nuclei undergoing HR-mediated repair, i.e., RAD51-positive nuclei, out of all S-G2 phase nuclei. As per previously established cut-off values, chemo-naïve samples with fHR score of <10 were classified as functionally HR-deficient, whereas samples with fHR score of >10 were classified as functionally HR-proficient. For chemo-treated samples, a cut-off of <30 was used for HR deficiency.

SNP-array analysis instructions
Before starting your analysis, check that the language of your computer is English. 
Then change decimal separator to ‘’.’’ instead of ‘’,’’.
Before starting the analysis, create a folder and name it by the date and the project (see example in L-drive: L:\ltdk_farkkila\Projects\SNP-arrays_results\Raw_and_intermediante_files_fernando\Oncosys_and_others )
Inside the forder, create a Raw_data folder and put the raw data inside. 
Create a manifest file in R-friendly version by
Create a new project in GenomeStudio
Install GenomeStudio 2.0 and open the app
Press “Genotyping” to create a new project
A new window called “GenomeStudio Project Wizard” pops out and press “Next”
Fill projects Repository space by selecting the file you have saved / want to save your documents regarding this project
Name your project and press “Next”
Choose “Use sample sheet to load sample intensities” and press “Next”
See screenshot below. Select Sample sheet you received from Tartu
Fill Data repository by selecting the Raw_data folder you created
Fill Manifest repository by selecting the folder where your manifest file (.bpm) is. This file is specific for each kind of SNP-array (Currently there is one available for our project here: L:\ltdk_farkkila\Projects\SNP-arrays_results\Utils\Manifest_Tartu
Press “Next”
Put a tab to “Import cluster positions from a cluster file”. The cluster file is also specific for each kind of SNP-array. Currently there is one available for our project here: L:\ltdk_farkkila\Projects\SNP-arrays_results\Utils\Cluster_file\Clusters_GenomeStudio_project.egt
Choose your cluster file (.egt)
Press “Finish”
Do you wish to do the SNP statistics to all the SNPs? Select yes
Now your project is being created to GenomeStudio

Here an example that shows how to manipulate the “Full data table”: link 
Calculate Call rates on the Sample table by pressing the calculator icon
Then press ‘’select all’’ and export the "Sample Table" to a file, name the file: ““Samples_QC.txt””
Select columns with column chooser from Full Data Table in this order: 
Name
Chr
Positon
LogR ratio
In order to export the Full Data Table, do not press anything on the table and then press “Select all” icon on the left side of the tool bar
Name the file as LogRratio_samples.txt
It will ask you if you want to export all the columns say ‘’no\\
Exporting might take time
Export BAF sample file by selecting columns Name, Chr, Position and Ballele Freq in this order
Do the same to B allele file and name it as BAF_samples.txt
Further info:
This is a nice tutorial about how to perform quality check in GenomeStudio and how-to identify the allelic imbalances: link. In particular these are the most useful tutorials: "GenomeStudio Genotyping: Evaluating Infinium Assay Controls" & "CNV Analysis Basics".

How to get access to the data:
Download WinSCP from Software center (if you don’t have it on your computer already) and open it. 
You have received an email from Tartu after the SNP-array is done. You will get access to the data by using the log in information in the email. For the address part use a number code in brackets.
The WinSCP window you just opened is divided into two: on the left side can be seen your computer’s documents and on the right side is server to Tartu documents. Open the file from the left side, where you want to download the documents. And then open the files on the right side and choose the project folder and press download. This will download the files from Tartu to the folder you selected. This might take some time.
